title: I Do Not Wish to Enter a Contest of Abusive Words!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation, removes one OCR-only junk line, and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors encountered during translation. The canonical source document remains unchanged. The wordplay built around `அரைவேக்காடு` is carried into English as "half-baked," with its escalating variants preserved. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: There is a certain civility expected when someone holding the office of Chief Minister speaks or issues a statement. Yet Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa is the very person who keeps disgracing that office. Arrogant in the knowledge that she holds power, and proud that a few newspapers receive full-page advertisements to publish her statements in full, she seems to think she may write whatever she likes. She has issued yet another lengthy statement on river-water disputes. As usual, she claims that I issued a hollow statement hoping for political gain and was made to suffer for challenging her. If one scoops up sewage with one's hand, how can the hand remain clean?

On 7 July 2014, Jayalalithaa replied to the letter I wrote on 30 June under the title "Who Is Half-Baked?" As usual, she dragged me into a needless quarrel. I had referred to Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy's statement in the Kerala Assembly, reported in the newspapers, that the Mullaperiyar, Parambikulam, Peruvaripallam and Thunakkadavu dams were reclassified in 2012 as belonging to Kerala. I asked whether Tamil Nadu should not state its response. Instead of answering the Kerala Chief Minister, Jayalalithaa assumed I had asked out of ignorance and replied by crudely calling me "half-baked."
That is why I offered an explanation and asked: who is half-baked, me or Jayalalithaa?

When a *Dinamani* reporter in Delhi asked the Kerala Chief Minister about Jayalalithaa's response, he again stated categorically that although Tamil Nadu maintains the four dams, it cannot claim ownership over them; that all four are recorded as belonging to Kerala in the National Register of Large Dams; and that there is no question of compromise on the matter. Therefore, on 1 July itself, I said that the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister owed an answer to the Kerala Chief Minister, and had no reason to become angry with me or vent her rage at me.

Without even reading this, the Chief Minister's statement of the 7th lunges at me as though I were the one who said the dams belonged to Kerala. It was Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy who said, not once but twice, that the four dams belonged to Kerala. If Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has courage, strength and justice on her side, she should answer Oommen Chandy. What use is there in raging against me? I merely asked whether Tamil Nadu should respond when the Kerala Chief Minister had said all this. Was that wrong? Do we now understand who is half-baked? Indeed, has Jayalalithaa's statement not established that she is not even half-baked, but only quarter-baked, never having come to the boil at all? What political gain is there for me in this?

Next, as usual, Jayalalithaa repeated in her statement the same accusations she always levels against me over the Cauvery dispute, Katchatheevu and Mullaperiyar. I have answered those accusations repeatedly. I do not wish to recite all of it yet again. One can wake those who are asleep, but can one wake those who only pretend to sleep?

In her statement of 29 June 2014, Jayalalithaa said, "The Supreme Court delivered a judgment in February 2012 in the public-interest litigation concerning the interlinking of rivers." In my response of 30 June, I pointed out: "Even if Jayalalithaa concealed in her statement that this Supreme Court judgment resulted from the case filed in the Madras High Court in 1983 by Kazhagam advocate K. S. Radhakrishnan and his subsequent appeal to the Supreme Court, those familiar with river interlinking will not forget it."

Unable to answer this directly, Jayalalithaa asked how a case filed by K. S. Radhakrishnan in his individual capacity could be treated as one filed by the DMK. It is enough if she at least admits that the case was not filed by the AIADMK or by Jayalalithaa! She has also made the new discovery that Radhakrishnan was not in the DMK when he filed it. Whatever party he belonged to then, is he not in the DMK now? That is why I referred to him in my statement as Kazhagam advocate Radhakrishnan.

Those who wrote Jayalalithaa's statement searched through Radhakrishnan's books and articles and quoted criticism he made of me while he was in another party. Poor Chief Minister! Those who drafted her statement should not have humiliated the head of a State in this way. Even in that article, Radhakrishnan stated the truth that "Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Karunanidhi is calling upon Delhi to interlink the rivers," thereby confirming that I had urged the Union government even then to undertake river interlinking.

Jayalalithaa again claims that Karunanidhi did not lift even a twig to implement river interlinking. Yet, without realising it, her own statement describes what I did. For example, she says that in 2008 the Tamil Nadu government sent the Union government a request for financial assistance for the project linking the Cheyyar with the Pennaiyar. I was Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu in 2008 when that request was made.

The DMK government's 2007-08 Budget itself stated: "To prevent river-water sharing disputes from arising in a manner that harms the unity, integrity and sovereignty of India's various States, this government will continue vigorous efforts to ensure that the Union government immediately begins, as an urgent necessity, the work of interlinking at least the peninsular rivers." Following this, at the National Development Council meeting in Delhi, I spoke at length to draw the attention of the Union government and every State government to river interlinking.

We did not stop with a Budget announcement. To prevent flood danger, keep floodwater from being wasted in the sea and carry water to drought-stricken areas, the DMK government made Tamil Nadu the first State in India to undertake a project linking rivers flowing within a State.

Under the Cauvery-Gundar linking project to take surplus water to dry districts, work was approved and undertaken on a barrage across the Cauvery at Kattalai near Mayanur at an estimated cost of 189 crore rupees. The Thamirabarani-Karumeniyar-Nambiyar linking project was approved at an estimated cost of 369 crore rupees and work was undertaken. A 174-crore-rupee proposal to link the Cheyyar with the Pennaiyar was sent to the Union government's Water Commission for consideration.
The people of Tamil Nadu must understand the deception involved in concealing all these facts and then claiming that Karunanidhi did not lift even a twig for river interlinking.

Let her present arguments; I have not asked her not to. There is nothing wrong with those in politics putting forward their respective cases. But should there not be some civility and culture in a person holding the office of Chief Minister? Does any Chief Minister in any other State scatter words in this manner? When I responsibly ask whether the Tamil Nadu government should respond to what the Kerala Chief Minister has said, she ignores him and, while holding the office of Chief Minister, calls me half-baked and says I invited my own humiliation. She may consider that admirable. I am ashamed to answer in the same manner.

I have practised politics with civility alongside Thanthai Periyar, Perarignar Anna, Rajaji, Kamaraj, the elder statesman Bhaktavatsalam, the respected Quaid-e-Millath and even MGR; and at the national level with Mother Indira Gandhi, Sonia Gandhi and Manmohan Singh. Having done so, I am not prepared now to reply in language resembling a quarrel at a public water tap.

Do the people not know how Jayalalithaa has criticised not only me and Tamil Nadu's leaders, but leaders across India? Did she not call Navalar Nedunchezhian a "shed hair"? Did she not accuse Advani of "selective amnesia"? Did she not call Narasimha Rao a "non-functioning Prime Minister"? Did she not state in the Assembly that Governor Channa Reddy had tried to misbehave with her? Did she not say that the "tin can" was invented only for Communists to rattle while collecting donations? This may be an art Jayalalithaa has mastered. As far as I am concerned, I do not wish to enter this uncivilised contest of words with her.

I rejected Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy's words and argued for the people of Tamil Nadu. Without even the capacity to understand that, Jayalalithaa has needlessly thrown dust upon me and abused me. I ask fair-minded readers to consider, after reading this, whether that is just, and to deliver their verdict.
With affection,
M. K.
9 July 2014
